title: Numbers in the Night
summary: Sam gets strange phone calls with numbers every night at nine.
coverScene: a boy holding an old telephone by a window at night while a soft white light crosses the starry sky

sentences.text: Sam lives with his father.
Their house is old and small.
In the house there is an old phone.
It is on the wall by the door.
No one calls it any more.
One night at nine, the phone sounds.
Sam answers it.
Someone says numbers.
"One hundred," says the man.
Then the call ends.
Sam looks at the phone a long time.
The next night, the phone sounds again.
It is nine again.
The same man says: "Ninety-nine."
The night after that: "Ninety-eight."
Every night the number goes down.
Sam tells his father.
"It is a work call," says his father.
"Or someone plays with you."
But Sam writes every number on paper.
He understands the numbers.
They count down to something.
One night, Sam asks a question.
"Who are you?" he says.
The man is quiet for a little time.
Then he says: "I am far away."
"I am coming home."
"Please wait for me."
The numbers go down, night after night.
Ten. Nine. Eight.
Sam waits by the phone every night.
"Three," says the man. "Two."
Then comes the last night.
At nine, the phone says nothing.
Sam waits and waits.
Then a big light goes over the town.
It is white and very beautiful.
It goes down slowly, like a star.
Then it is night again.
The phone makes one small sound.
Sam answers it fast.
"I am home," says the man.
"Thank you for waiting."
The calls never come again.
Now Sam looks at the night a lot.
And sometimes he smiles.

quiz.q: When does the old phone sound?
What does the man say every night?
What happens on the last night?
options: A big light goes over the town

sentences.text: Sam lived with his father in an old house.
On the wall by the door there was an old phone.
It came with the house, black and heavy.
Nobody called it any more.
"It is older than me," his father liked to say.
One night at exactly nine, the old phone started to sound.
Sam and his father looked at each other.
Sam answered it first.
A man's voice said one word: "One hundred."
Then the line went quiet.
The voice sounded very far away, and a little tired.
The next night at nine, the phone sounded again.
"Ninety-nine," said the same far voice.
The night after that, it said: "Ninety-eight."
It never said anything more, and it never missed a night.
His father said it was a game or a work machine.
But he started to stand near the phone at nine too.
Sam wrote every number in a little book.
After two weeks, he understood it.
The numbers were counting down to something.
But to what?
He looked at the night from his window and thought about it.
One night, Sam spoke before the voice could.
"Who are you?" he asked. "What are the numbers?"
The line was quiet for a long moment.
"I am very far away," the voice said at last.
"I have been away for a very long time."
"I am coming home. Please wait for me."
Then it said: "Forty-one," and the line went quiet.
After that, Sam never missed a call.
He sat by the phone every night with his little book.
The voice sounded closer now, night by night.
"Ten," it said one evening, and Sam's heart went fast.
"Three. Two. One."
Then came the night of zero.
At nine, the phone stayed quiet.
Sam and his father waited by the window without talking.
At ten past nine, a great white light crossed over the town.
It came down slowly, like a star that had found its place.
The glasses on the table sang a little song.
Then the night was black and quiet again.
A minute later, the old phone made one soft sound.
Sam answered before it could make another.
"I am home," said the voice, and it sounded very near now.
"Thank you for waiting. Thank you for answering."
The old phone never sounded again after that night.
But Sam still looks at the night sky, and sometimes he smiles.

quiz.q: What was strange about the calls?
options: The voice only said a number, one less every night
The calls came in the morning

quiz.q: What did Sam do with the numbers?
options: He wrote them in a little book

quiz.q: What did the voice ask Sam to do?

sentences.text: The old phone came with the house, like the doors and the garden.
It hung on the wall by the kitchen, black and heavy and silent.
Sam's father kept it because throwing it away felt somehow wrong.
"It is older than both of us together," he used to say.
Nobody had called it in years, and nobody knew its number.
Sam liked its weight and its patient face, the way you like a sleeping animal.
That changed on a Tuesday night at exactly nine o'clock.
The old phone rang so suddenly that Sam dropped his fork.
He reached it first and lifted the heavy black handle.
A man's voice, distant and tired, said a single word: "One hundred."
Then the line clicked and went dead.
Sam stood there holding the phone long after the sound had ended.
The call returned the next night at nine, and every night after.
"Ninety-nine," the voice said. Then "ninety-eight", then "ninety-seven".
It never explained, never answered, never missed a single night.
His father tried tracing the number, but the line showed no caller at all.
"A machine somewhere," he decided. "A broken computer counting to nothing."
But Sam noticed that his father was always near the phone at nine.
Sam kept a notebook and recorded every number with its date.
The pattern was perfect: one number less each night, no exceptions.
If the counting continued, it would reach zero in early spring.
Something was going to happen then, he was sure of it.
He began watching the night sky from his window, though he could not say why.
He told his best friend nothing; some things ask to be kept.
In February, Sam finally found the courage to interrupt.
"Who are you?" he asked quickly, before the number could come.
The silence lasted so long that he thought the line had died.
"I am very far away," the voice said finally, and it sounded almost happy.
"Further than you can imagine. I have been traveling home for a long time."
"Your phone is the only one that still remembers how to listen."
"Please keep answering. It helps me find the way. Forty-one."
After that night, Sam treated nine o'clock as a promise.
He answered through school nights, sick nights, and one long power cut.
The voice grew slowly clearer, as if the distance itself were shrinking.
His father stopped calling it a broken machine and started making tea for two at nine.
At school, Sam counted hours; at home, he counted nights.
"Ten," the voice said one evening, and neither of them slept well.
Three nights later it said: "One." And then came the night of zero.
At nine o'clock, for the first time in months, the phone stayed silent.
Sam and his father stood at the window, watching the dark without a word.
At ten past nine, a white light crossed the sky above the town.
It moved slowly and came down beyond the hills, soft as a falling star.
The windows sang quietly in their frames, and the night went still again.
Far away a dog called once, and then even the dogs went quiet.
One minute later, the old phone rang one soft, short ring.
"I am home," said the voice, and it sounded like someone smiling.
"Thank you for waiting. Thank you for every single answer."
The line went quiet forever after that, and the phone never rang again.
They never learned who he was, or what had come down beyond the hills.
But on clear nights, Sam still watches the sky, and he always will.

quiz.q: Why did the father's 'broken machine' theory weaken?
options: The line showed no caller and he began waiting by the phone himself
The phone company explained the calls

quiz.q: What did the voice say about Sam's phone?
options: It was the only one that still remembered how to listen
It was the oldest phone in the country
It belonged to the voice long ago

quiz.q: What happened on the night of zero?
options: A white light crossed the sky and came down beyond the hills
The phone rang one hundred times

sentences.text: The phone had been hanging on the kitchen wall since before Sam was born.
It was a heavy black instrument from another age, kept the way some families keep a grandfather's chair.
The line still worked, technically, though nobody had dialed the number in living memory.
Sam's father maintained that old machines deserved to retire in place, not in boxes.
So it hung there, polished and silent, a small museum of missed conversations.
Visitors sometimes asked why they kept it, and his father always gave the same answer.
"Because it still works," he would say, "and working things deserve a wall."
It rang for the first time on a Tuesday in November, at nine o'clock exactly.
The sound was so unexpected that Sam's father knocked his cup off the table.
Sam reached the phone first and lifted the cold, heavy handle to his ear.
Through a soft storm of distance and static, a man's voice pronounced one word: "One hundred."
The line clicked politely and died, leaving Sam with his own heartbeat.
He stood there so long that his father gently took the handle from him and hung it up.
The calls arrived nightly after that, punctual to the minute.
"Ninety-nine." "Ninety-eight." "Ninety-seven." Nothing more was ever offered.
His father called the phone company, who reported, with some discomfort, that no calls were being received at all.
According to their records, the old line had been disconnected for eleven years.
"A crossed wire," his father concluded, in the voice of a man convincing himself.
Yet by December he had rearranged his evenings so that nine o'clock always found him in the kitchen.
Sam checked the wall calendar every morning, as though the date might have moved in the night.
Sam approached the mystery the only way he knew: with a notebook and stubbornness.
Every number was recorded with its date, and the pattern held without a single exception.
One number less each night meant zero would arrive on the third of March.
He marked the date in red and told nobody why.
Without quite deciding to, he began spending his evenings watching the sky.
The notebook filled slowly, one line a night, like a diary written by someone very far from home.
Winter tightened around the town while the numbers marched calmly downward.
At school, Sam sat through lessons with the distracted face of a boy keeping a schedule nobody else could see.
He considered telling his best friend and decided against it, twice.
A secret shared is a secret spent, and this one still had work to do.
At home, the ritual settled into the shape of family life.
Dinner moved earlier, homework finished by half past eight, and the kitchen fell respectfully quiet before nine.
Neither of them called it what it was, which is how families handle miracles.
In February, curiosity finally defeated caution.
"Who are you?" Sam asked, the moment the line opened. "What are you counting?"
The pause that followed was so long that he apologized into the silence.
"I am very far away," the voice said at last, and there was warmth inside the tiredness.
"Farther than your maps go. I have been traveling home for longer than you have been alive."
"Old lines like yours are lighthouses. Yours is the last one still burning."
"Keep answering, if you are willing. Every answer is a light. Forty-one."
From that night on, answering became a duty that Sam ranked above homework and sleep.
He answered with a fever, once, and once by candlelight during a power cut that spared, mysteriously, the old line.
The voice grew steadily nearer, losing its static the way a swimmer loses the sea.
His father surrendered his theories one by one and took up making tea for the occasion instead.
"Ten," said the voice one evening, and the kitchen felt like a station platform.
The last three nights passed like held breath: three, two, one.
It was the kind of waiting that makes a house feel like a held breath.
On the third of March, nine o'clock came and went in silence.
Sam and his father stood shoulder to shoulder at the window, saying nothing.
At ten past nine, a white light drew a slow, deliberate line across the sky.
It was not falling; it was arriving, the difference somehow unmistakable.
It settled beyond the hills without a sound, and the window glass sang one thin note.
Then the ordinary night closed over the town like water over a stone.
Exactly one minute later, the old phone rang a single soft ring.
They did not move, and they did not speak, and the tea went cold behind them.
"I am home," the voice said, close and clear, like a neighbor across a fence.
"Thank you for waiting. Thank you for every one of the fifty-nine answers."
"You kept a light burning for a stranger. That is rarer than you know."
The line closed gently, and the old phone has not made a sound since.
The hills kept their usual shape, and the town went on selling bread and complaining about the weather.
They never spoke of it to anyone, and nothing was ever reported about the hills.
But the phone stays polished on the kitchen wall.
And on clear nights Sam watches the sky with the patience of a lighthouse keeper.
Somewhere out there, he is sure, another count has already begun for somebody else.

quiz.q: What did the phone company report about the calls?
options: No calls were being received; the line had been disconnected for years
The calls came from another country
The calls were a paid advertisement

quiz.q: What did the voice compare old phone lines to?
How did the light in the sky differ from a falling star?
options: It was arriving deliberately, not falling
It was red instead of white
